title: Fall of Tenochtitlán
summary: On 13 August 1521, after a seventy-nine-day siege, Hernán Cortés and his Spanish and indigenous allies captured the young Mexica emperor Cuauhtémoc as he tried to escape by canoe across Lake Texcoco. The great island city of Tenochtitlán, capital of the Aztec Triple Alliance and one of the largest urban centres in the world, lay in ruins around him. In two years the alliance that had ruled central Mexico for a century was dismantled, and the foundations of the Spanish viceroyalty of New Spain were laid on the rubble.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The city Cortés attacked in 1521 was less than two centuries old and unlike anything the Spaniards had seen. Founded on a marshy island in Lake Texcoco around 1325, Tenochtitlán by the reign of Moctezuma II had grown into a network of causeways, chinampa fields, and stone palaces housing perhaps two hundred thousand people, with tributaries stretching from the Gulf of Mexico to the Pacific. Its central precinct held the twin pyramid of Huitzilopochtli and Tlaloc, a great ball court, and the tzompantli skull rack; its Tlatelolco market was said to hold sixty thousand people on a busy day. Political power was shared, uneasily, among Tenochtitlán, Texcoco, and Tlacopan in the Triple Alliance, but the Mexica of Tenochtitlán had come to dominate.

That power rested on continual tribute war. Provinces sent maize, cacao, cotton mantles, gold, and captives for sacrifice, and unreconciled peoples such as the Tlaxcaltec confederation in the eastern highlands resented the alliance intensely. In February 1519 Hernán Cortés, a lawyer's clerk turned encomendero from Extremadura, sailed from Cuba against the orders of the governor Diego Velázquez with about five hundred men, sixteen horses, and a handful of small cannon. He landed at Cempoala on the Gulf coast in April, scuttled his ships to prevent retreat, and marched inland. At Tlaxcala he fought and then negotiated with the four Tlaxcaltec lords, who saw in him a chance to strike back at the Mexica.

He also acquired the woman known variously as Malintzin, Marina, and La Malinche, a Nahua noblewoman gifted to the Spaniards after the battle of Centla and fluent in Nahuatl and Maya. With her and the Spanish castaway Gerónimo de Aguilar he could speak, negotiate, and gather intelligence. Cortés entered Tenochtitlán on 8 November 1519 along the Iztapalapa causeway, welcomed with formal courtesy by Moctezuma II, and within days took the emperor into a form of house arrest inside the palace of his father Axayacatl.

The balance held for six months. Then, in May 1520, Velázquez sent Pánfilo de Narváez with a larger force to arrest Cortés; Cortés marched back to the coast, defeated Narváez by night at Cempoala, and recruited most of his men. While he was away, his lieutenant Pedro de Alvarado ordered the massacre of unarmed nobles dancing at the festival of Toxcatl in the great precinct. When Cortés returned to a besieged palace, Moctezuma was killed under disputed circumstances; on the night of 30 June to 1 July, remembered as the Noche Triste, the Spaniards fought their way out along the Tlacopan causeway and lost perhaps six hundred men and most of their treasure.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The Mexica did not press their advantage. Cortés and his surviving men, wounded but alive, reached the friendly city of Tlaxcala and spent the second half of 1520 rebuilding. Meanwhile a catastrophe fell on the Mexica that no army could hold off. Smallpox, apparently brought ashore in April 1520 by an enslaved African in Narváez's company, spread from Cempoala to the highlands and reached Tenochtitlán by autumn. The disease raged for about sixty days. It killed the emperor Cuitláhuac, Moctezuma's brother, after a reign of only eighty days; contemporary chroniclers described bodies piled in the streets, whole households starving because no one could rise to fetch water. Modern estimates suggest it carried off between a quarter and forty per cent of the population of central Mexico.

By the spring of 1521 Cortés had assembled a new force: perhaps nine hundred Spanish infantry, eighty-six horsemen, and sixteen guns, alongside tens of thousands of Tlaxcaltec, Cholulteca, Texcocan, and other allies. He had also built thirteen shallow-draft brigantines, sawn as timber in Tlaxcala, carried in pieces over the mountains, and assembled on the shore of Lake Texcoco. On 26 May 1521 he cut the aqueduct from Chapultepec that supplied the city with fresh water. Three Spanish columns, commanded by Cortés, Alvarado, and Gonzalo de Sandoval, took the causeways from Tlacopan, Coyoacán, and Iztapalapa; the brigantines dispersed the Mexica war canoes on the lake.

The siege lasted seventy-nine days and turned into a house-by-house destruction of the city. The new tlatoani, Cuauhtémoc, a young cousin of Moctezuma crowned in February, refused every summons to surrender. Spanish gunners levelled the great buildings of the central precinct so that cavalry could operate; Tlaxcaltec sappers pulled down houses at the water's edge and filled the canals with rubble so that the Spanish could advance. Starvation and thirst reduced the defenders to eating tree bark and leather, but they fought on and on 30 June, one year to the day after the Noche Triste, they inflicted a heavy defeat on Cortés's column near the marketplace of Tlatelolco and captured perhaps sixty Spaniards, whose sacrifices atop the great pyramid the survivors could watch from a distance.

On 13 August 1521 the last resistance collapsed in Tlatelolco. Cuauhtémoc, trying to slip out across the lake in a canoe with his family and a few nobles, was intercepted by Sandoval's brigantines and brought to Cortés on a rooftop overlooking the ruined market. Cortés received him with formal courtesy. The tlatoani asked to be killed with the dagger at Cortés's belt; instead he was kept alive as a prisoner and, four years later, hanged during Cortés's disastrous expedition to Honduras on suspicion of conspiracy. Casualty estimates for the siege vary widely; the Mexica may have lost more than a hundred thousand dead from combat, disease, and starvation, and the surviving population was scattered.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Cortés kept his allies' anger from becoming a general massacre only imperfectly; a slaughter in the marketplace and canals on the day of surrender was remembered with horror in later indigenous chronicles. He founded a Spanish city, Mexico, on the rubble of the ceremonial precinct, ordered the great temple demolished, and began building a cathedral on its site. The lake was gradually drained over the following centuries. Charles V made him marqués del Valle de Oaxaca and granted him vast estates, but denied him the governorship of what became the viceroyalty of New Spain in 1535; Cortés spent the last years of his life quarrelling with the crown and died in Spain in 1547.

The collapse of the Triple Alliance did not extinguish indigenous political life. Local Nahua altepetl continued to function under Spanish supervision; the sons and daughters of Mexica and Texcocan nobles were baptised, given Spanish names, and sometimes granted encomiendas of their own. Tlaxcala, rewarded for its role in the conquest, retained a special exempt status for centuries. But the encomienda system, in which Spaniards received the labour and tribute of indigenous communities, together with epidemic disease, mining conscription, and the great cocoliztli mortalities of the 1540s and 1570s, reduced the native population of central Mexico from perhaps twenty million on the eve of contact to little more than one million by 1620.

Worldwide the consequences were profound. Silver from the mines discovered at Zacatecas in 1546 and Potosí in Upper Peru in 1545 flowed through Mexico City to Manila and thence to Ming China, integrating the Americas into a single early modern economy and setting off inflation in Europe. Maize, chocolate, tomatoes, and chillies went east; wheat, sugar cane, horses, cattle, and pathogens went west. The Franciscan and Dominican missions that followed the conquistadors began an ambitious and violent evangelisation, producing along the way the vast ethnographic works of Bernardino de Sahagún, whose Nahua informants gave the first indigenous account of the fall of Tenochtitlán in books preserved as the Florentine Codex.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The fall of Tenochtitlán has been retold in every generation since, and its meaning has always been contested. For sixteenth-century Spanish chroniclers such as Francisco López de Gómara it was a providential victory that opened a new Christian world; for the friar Bartolomé de las Casas, who spent his long life denouncing the cruelty of the conquistadors, it was proof of the humanity of indigenous peoples and the sinfulness of the empire that ruled them. Nahuatl-language accounts, composed by former Mexica and Texcocan nobles, recorded a shattered city in terms of memory and lament: 'broken spears lie in the roads,' one of the poems begins.

In independent Mexico after 1821 Cuauhtémoc was reclaimed as a founding martyr. A colossal statue of him was erected on the Paseo de la Reforma in Mexico City in 1887; the last Mexica tlatoani has appeared on banknotes, in murals by Diego Rivera and David Alfaro Siqueiros, and in the toponyms of a dozen states. Cortés, by contrast, is scarcely commemorated in Mexico; his remains rest in a small church in the capital rather than any monument. Malintzin's memory is more ambivalent still, alternately damned as a traitor and honoured as a mother of the mixed mestizo people that eventually gave Mexico its national identity.

Modern historians have moved decisively away from the older heroic account of a few hundred Spaniards conquering an empire. The work of Matthew Restall, Camilla Townsend, Susan Schroeder, and many others has emphasised the enormous role of indigenous allies, the decisive impact of Old World disease, and the political fractures within the Mexica state itself. The five-hundredth anniversary in 2021, marked by ceremonies at the ruins of the Templo Mayor and speeches by President Andrés Manuel López Obrador demanding an apology from Spain, showed that the memory of 1521 is still being negotiated on both sides of the Atlantic.

worldContext.text: While Cortés was tightening his siege of Tenochtitlán the young Ottoman sultan Suleiman I opened his own reign with the capture of Belgrade on 29 August 1521, breaking the Hungarian frontier that had held for a century. Rhodes, defended by the Knights Hospitaller, would fall to him in December 1522. In Cairo, only four years after Selim I's conquest, Ottoman governors were adjusting the administration of Egypt and rerouting the Red Sea spice trade toward Istanbul. Safavid Persia under Shah Ismail I was rebuilding after Chaldiran and would soon pass to his ten-year-old son Tahmasp.

In Ming China the Zhengde Emperor died on 20 April 1521 without an heir; his cousin, the fourteen-year-old Jiajing Emperor, was enthroned on 27 May and began a forty-five-year reign that would be dominated by Daoist ritual and factional court struggle. In April and May a Portuguese squadron under Diogo Calvo skirmished with Ming imperial fleets off Tuen Mun near the mouth of the Pearl River in the first clashes between European ships and East Asian navies. Japan, still in the Sengoku warring states period, was divided among daimyō who had never heard of Cortés.

In north India the Delhi Sultanate under Ibrahim Lodi was in its last years; disaffected Afghan nobles were already writing to the Timurid prince Babur in Kabul, and the invasion that would end the sultanate at Panipat in 1526 was being prepared. Portuguese Goa, taken by Afonso de Albuquerque in 1510, was the hub of an Estado da India that stretched from Sofala to Malacca and would receive Ferdinand Magellan's surviving ship, the Victoria, at Timor in 1522.

The Songhai Empire under Askia Muhammad, now old and blind, was in the middle of a long dynastic struggle among his sons that would depose him in 1528. In the Kingdom of Kongo the Christian king Afonso I was writing anguished letters to Lisbon protesting the accelerating Portuguese slave trade from his coast. Ethiopia's emperor Lebna Dengel presided over a Christian kingdom uneasy about the growing power of the Adal Sultanate on its Muslim frontier; the war of Ahmad Gragn was still a decade away.

The young Habsburg emperor Charles V, ruling Spain, the Low Countries, Austria, and now much of the Americas, spent the summer of 1521 opening war with Francis I of France in the Italian Wars, while his cousin Ferdinand fortified Vienna against the Turks. In April at the Diet of Worms Charles had condemned Martin Luther as an outlaw of the Empire, but Luther, sheltered by the Elector of Saxony, was already translating the New Testament into German at the Wartburg. English divorce politics under Henry VIII were still fifteen years from breaking with Rome.

Ferdinand Magellan's expedition, having crossed the Pacific from South America, reached Guam on 6 March 1521 and Cebu in the Philippines a fortnight later; Magellan himself was killed on 27 April at Mactan by warriors of the datu Lapulapu. The surviving ships pressed on to the Spice Islands, and only one, the Victoria under Juan Sebastián Elcano, would complete the first circumnavigation of the globe in September 1522. In Aotearoa Māori communities were expanding their pā fortifications and kūmara cultivation; in mainland Australia, Aboriginal nations continued their thousand-generations-old management of Country.

figures.name: Hernán Cortés
figures.role: Spanish captain from Extremadura who led the conquest of the Aztec Triple Alliance

figures.name: Cuauhtémoc
figures.role: Last independent tlatoani of Tenochtitlán, crowned February 1521 and captured 13 August

figures.name: Moctezuma II
figures.role: Mexica emperor who received Cortés in November 1519 and died in Spanish custody in June 1520

figures.name: Malintzin (La Malinche)
figures.role: Nahua noblewoman who served as Cortés's interpreter and adviser

figures.name: Pedro de Alvarado
figures.role: Cortés's captain whose massacre at the Festival of Toxcatl in 1520 triggered the uprising

images.caption: The Conquest of Mexico, an anonymous painting from the second half of the seventeenth century depicting the Spanish assault on Tenochtitlán from the causeways and the lake.
